text: Technological advances have enabled us to profile multiple omics layers with spatial information, significantly enhancing spatial domain detection and advancing a variety of biomedical research fields. Moreover, this approach offers profound insights into the interactions between intracellular and intercellular molecular mechanisms involved in the development, physiology, and pathogenesis of human diseases. In this comprehensive review, we examine current advancements in multi-omics technologies, focusing on their evolution and refinement over the past decade, including improvements in throughput and resolution, modality integration, and accuracy. We also discuss the pivotal contributions of spatial multi-omics in revealing spatial heterogeneity, constructing detailed spatial atlases, deciphering spatial crosstalk in tumor immunology, and advancing translational research and cancer therapy through precise spatial mapping.

Single-cell sequencing has been instrumental in providing detailed insights into gene expression at the individual cell level for decades. This technique has revealed the complexity of cellular diversity, exacerbated by processes such as cell proliferation, differentiation, and death, particularly in relation to the local and distant environment of the cell. Single-cell sequencing can detect cellular heterogeneity, enabling detailed analysis of individual cell behavior, mechanisms, and relationships. The high resolution of these methods has allowed for the extensive exploration and characterization of cell diversity on a large scale. However, despite these advantages, single-cell sequencing often fails to retain critical spatial information about cell populations, resulting in the loss of crucial spatial context. To overcome this limitation, spatial multi-omics has emerged as a transformative technology, enabling the precise localization of cells within tissues and the quantitative measurement of gene expression in situ. This advancement marks an important technological breakthrough in life sciences and biomedicine, with wide-ranging applications in neuroscience, developmental biology, and cancer research. Furthermore, spatial multi-omics allows researchers to investigate the development of multicellular organisms from single totipotent cells, as well as their function, aging, and disease progression. High-throughput multi-omics technologies, such as genomics, epigenomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, and metabolomics, have also facilitated the mapping of diverse molecular layers, significantly broadening the scope of biological analysis and our understanding of complex biological systems.

section: Technologies for Spatial Omics - Spatial Transcriptomics
text: Spatial transcriptomics has significantly enhanced our understanding of cellular organization and intra-tissue interactions based on the systematic measurement of gene expression levels across tissue space. Recent advancements in spatial transcriptomics sequencing have focused on increasing the number of detectable genes or proteins, enhancing sensitivity and resolution, simplifying operation, and expanding the size of the analyzed area. Spatial transcriptomics has been used in various fields, including cancer research, developmental biology, and disease studies. Fundamentally, spatial transcriptomics technology has the ability to reveal the precise spatial localization of RNA molecules within tissues. Mainstream spatial transcriptomics research strategies include image-based in situ transcriptomics and oligonucleotide-based spatial barcoding followed by next-generation sequencing (NGS). Image-based methods primarily include fluorescence in situ hybridization (smFISH) and in situ sequencing (ISS). smFISH uses reverse complementary oligo probes conjugated with fluorophores for precise mRNA quantification and localization at the single-cell level. However, it suffers from low probe specificity, low hybridization efficiency, and high background fluorescence. Multiplexed error-robust fluorescence in situ hybridization (MERFISH) addresses optical crowding by reducing error rates, while sequential FISH (seqFISH) achieves high coding efficiency through sequential rounds of hybridization. Sequencing-based methods like FISSEQ, STARmap, and BaristaSeq utilize padlock probes and rolling circle amplification (RCA) to generate amplicons for sequencing, offering unbiased whole transcriptome coverage but sometimes suffering from low capture efficiency.

section: Technologies for Spatial Omics - Spatial Genomics & Proteomics
text: Spatial genomics examines the genome within its spatial context. Techniques like In Situ Genome Sequencing (IGS) and Slide-DNA-seq allow for the spatial mapping of paired-end sequences of the whole genome and fragmented genomic DNA, respectively. IGS enables simultaneous sequencing and imaging of genomes in complete biological samples, while Slide-DNA-seq offers high-resolution, high-sensitivity sequencing of DNA from intact tissues. Spatial proteomics investigates the spatial distribution of proteins, utilizing mass spectrometry (MS)-based methods and imaging-based methods. MS-based approaches distinguish compounds by relative molecular weight difference, offering cheap and self-service operation but low resolution. Imaging-based methods visualize proteins via affinity-based reagents or fluorescent fusion proteins, providing high sensitivity but low throughput. Recent advancements combine these, such as Deep Visual Proteomics (DVP), which links protein abundance with complex cellular phenotypes using AI-driven image analysis and laser microdissection.

section: Technologies for Spatial Omics - Spatial Epigenomics & Metabolomics
text: Spatial epigenomics explores modifications to DNA and chromatin structure, such as histone acetylation and methylation, which regulate gene activity. The CUT&Tag method inserts adaptors directly during chromatin cutting based on Tn5 transposase, offering simple operation, short experiment periods, and high signal-to-noise ratios. Spatial metabolomics focuses on the qualitative and quantitative analysis of low-molecular-weight metabolites within an organism. Targeted metabolomics analyzes specific subsets of compounds using techniques like Fourier Transform Mass Spectrometry (FT-MS) and Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR), which offer high accuracy and throughput. Untargeted metabolomics aims for global analysis using LC/MS, which separates metabolites based on migration rates but faces challenges with low separation rates and bias. The complexity of metabolite detection requires advanced computational analysis, such as CAMERA, to identify isotopes, adducts, and fragments.

The integration of spatial multi-omics aims to achieve spatially resolved whole-exome or whole-genome sequencing by combining various spatially resolved omics technologies. This integration marks the beginning of the era of molecular anatomy. Key integrations include: 1) Spatial Transcriptomics and (Epi)Genomics: Technologies like Spatial ATAC-RNA-seq and Spatial CUT&Tag-RNA-seq integrate the chemistry of spatial ATAC-seq or CUT&Tag with spatial transcriptomics on the same tissue section via deterministic co-barcoding. This allows for genome-wide co-mapping of the epigenome and transcriptome at the cellular level. 2) Spatial Proteomics and Transcriptomics: Platforms like Spatial Multi-Omics (SM-Omics) and Spatial CITE-seq enable the simultaneous profiling of epitopes and transcriptomes within single cells using DNA-barcoded antibodies and next-generation sequencing. 3) Spatial Transcriptomics and Metabolomics: Protocols like Spatial Multimodal Analysis (SMA) combine spatially resolved transcriptomics and mass spectrometry imaging (MSI) in a single tissue slice, revealing associations and heterogeneities between transcriptomes and metabolomes. 4) Spatial Transcriptomics, Genomics, and Proteomics: Multi-omics in situ pairwise sequencing (MiP-seq) is a high-throughput targeted in situ sequencing technique that simultaneously detects multiplexed DNA, RNA, proteins, and biomolecules at subcellular resolution.

Spatial multi-omics has broad applications in deciphering spatial-specific atlas production, understanding disease heterogeneity, tumor immunology crosstalk, lineage tracking, and therapeutic investigations. In constructing spatial atlases, it has been applied to generate comprehensive maps of the human maternal-fetal interface and cardiac remodeling, revealing spatially resolved insights into cellular zones of injury and repair. In deciphering spatial heterogeneity in diseases like cancer, integration of transcriptomics and proteomics has uncovered distinct molecular signatures in metastatic prostate cancer and glioblastoma, identifying prognostic markers like PPP1R12A and RPS14. Regarding tumor immunology, spatial multi-omics elucidates the crosstalk between tumor cells and the microenvironment, identifying distinct interfaces like peritumoral lymphoid tissue (PLT) with unique metabolic and inflammatory profiles. In lineage tracking, it helps visualize the continuum of cell state changes and differentiation trajectories, as seen in the diversification of mesenchymal states. Finally, in therapeutics, it aids in identifying metabolic vulnerabilities and potential targets, such as in triple-negative breast cancer where microbial metabolites like TMAO are found to induce pyroptosis and enhance anti-tumor immunity.

The document includes Table 1, which summarizes sub-technologies of spatial transcriptomics, spatial genomics, spatial proteomics, spatial epigenomics, and spatial metabolomics, detailing their analyte principles, features, limitations, and references. For instance, smFISH uses in situ hybridization of reverse-complementary oligo probes, offering precise localization but suffering from low probe specificity. Slide-DNA-seq uses in situ fragmentation of genomic DNA with barcodes for sequencing, noted for high resolution but low efficiency. Table 2 lists technologies for spatial multi-omics integration, such as Slide-DNA-seq (Integrating transcriptomics and genomics, 25μm resolution), Spatial ATAC-RNA-seq (Integrating epigenomics and transcriptomics, 20-25μm resolution), SM-Omics (Integrating proteomics and transcriptomics, 100μm resolution), and SMA (Integrating spatial transcriptomics and metabolomics, 10-55μm resolution). Table 3 outlines applications of integrated spatial-based technologies across diseases like metastatic prostate cancer, non-small cell lung cancer, glioblastoma, gastric cancer, and ulcerative colitis, detailing the integrated technologies used and the key findings.

The field of spatial omics technologies is rapidly evolving towards higher resolution, deeper coverage, greater multiplexity, and enhanced versatility in analyzing diverse samples, including formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded, fresh-frozen, and living tissues. Advancements enable 3D reconstruction of larger tissue regions and comprehensive analysis of spatiotemporal multi-omics. However, challenges remain in effectively acquiring, manipulating, analyzing, and visualizing complex datasets, necessitating specialized hardware and software. Key steps include normalizing data matrices, removing low-quality data, improving signal-to-noise ratios, and eliminating unwanted technical and biological variations. The development of independent benchmarks for spatial omics integration algorithms is crucial. Ultimately, multi-omics techniques with spatial single-cell resolution are anticipated to revolutionize our understanding of cell biology, offering profound insights into disease mechanisms, drug target identification, and biomarker discovery, thereby advancing personalized precision medicine.
